sections.title: Domain & Infrastructure Footprinting — Domain & DNS Records

items.title: Collect WHOIS / RDAP registration data
items.description: Pull WHOIS/RDAP for the apex domain and related domains to identify registrant org, registrar, name servers, and creation/expiry dates.
items.how_to_find:
Pull registration data for domains + IP ranges:

```
whois <domain>
curl -s https://rdap.org/domain/<domain> | jq
```

Note registrant emails, orgs, name servers, and registration dates.
items.how_to_fix: Use domain privacy/registrar redaction on WHOIS, use role-based (not personal) registrant contacts, and avoid leaking internal naming. Re-check WHOIS exposure.

items.title: Enumerate DNS records (A/AAAA/MX/NS/TXT/CNAME)
items.description: Query the full record set to discover mail servers, third-party SaaS via CNAMEs, and SPF/DKIM/DMARC TXT records that reveal infrastructure providers.
items.how_to_find:
Query all record types:

```
dig <domain> ANY +noall +answer
for t in A AAAA MX NS TXT CNAME SOA; do dig +short $t <domain>; done
```

TXT records often leak SaaS/verification tokens and infra hints.
items.how_to_fix: Publish only necessary DNS records, avoid leaking internal hostnames/infra in TXT, and review records periodically. Re-audit DNS.

items.title: Perform reverse DNS (PTR) lookups
items.description: Resolve IPs back to hostnames across the target's ranges to discover internal naming conventions and additional hosts on shared netblocks.
items.how_to_find:
Reverse-resolve the target's IP ranges to find related hosts:

```
for ip in $(seq 1 254); do dig +short -x <prefix>.$ip; done
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: avoid descriptive PTR names that reveal roles/environments, and review PTR records. Re-check reverse DNS.

items.title: Review historical DNS / passive DNS
items.description: Use passive DNS and historical resolution datasets to find prior IPs, retired subdomains, and origin servers hidden behind a CDN/WAF.
items.how_to_find:
Look at historical resolutions to find old infra / origin IPs:

```
# SecurityTrails / passivedns / VirusTotal passive DNS for <domain>
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: rotate/retire exposed IPs, and be aware historical DNS can reveal origin IPs behind a CDN. Re-review historical exposure.

items.title: Passively enumerate subdomains from OSINT sources
items.description: Aggregate subdomains from passive sources (search engines, threat intel feeds, DNS aggregators) without sending traffic to the target.
items.how_to_find:
Collect subdomains passively (no direct traffic):

```
subfinder -d <domain> -all -silent
amass enum -passive -d <domain>
```
items.how_to_fix: Maintain an authoritative subdomain inventory, decommission stale entries, and monitor for new/unknown subdomains. Re-enumerate to compare against inventory.

references.title: OWASP WSTG - Enumerate Applications on Webserver

items.title: Mine certificate transparency logs
items.description: Query CT logs (crt.sh, Censys, Google CT) to discover subdomains issued TLS certificates, including internal and pre-release hostnames.
items.how_to_find:
Pull hostnames from CT logs:

```
curl -s 'https://crt.sh/?q=%25.<domain>&output=json' | jq -r '.[].name_value' | sort -u
```
items.how_to_fix: Be aware CT logs publicly reveal all issued cert hostnames (including internal-looking ones); avoid descriptive names, and monitor CT for your domains. Re-review CT exposure.

items.title: Generate subdomain permutations from known patterns
items.description: Derive likely additional subdomains by permuting/altering discovered names (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Generate + resolve permutations of known subdomains:

```
gotator -sub subs.txt -perm words.txt | dnsx -silent
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: retire predictable/stale hosts and keep an inventory. Re-check which permutations resolve.

items.title: Flag dangling subdomains (takeover candidates)
items.description: Identify subdomains with CNAMEs pointing to unclaimed third-party services (deprovisioned S3, Heroku, GitHub Pages, etc.) as subdomain-takeover candidates.
items.how_to_find:
Find subdomains pointing to unclaimed services (CNAME to deprovisioned SaaS):

```
subzy run --targets subs.txt
nuclei -l subs.txt -t takeovers/
```

Dangling CNAME = subdomain-takeover risk (high).
items.how_to_fix: Remove dangling DNS records pointing to unclaimed resources, claim/decommission services properly, and monitor for takeover conditions. Re-scan to confirm none remain.

sections.title: Domain & Infrastructure Footprinting — IP Ranges, ASN & Exposed Services

items.title: Map ASN and owned IP ranges
items.description: Identify the Autonomous System Number(s) and netblocks registered to the org to scope owned IP space for the assessment.
items.how_to_find:
Find the org's ASN + netblocks:

```
whois -h whois.radb.net -- '-i origin AS<num>'
amass intel -asn <num>
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: know your own IP ranges, minimize public exposure, and monitor them. Re-verify ownership inventory.

items.title: Inventory exposed services via Shodan
items.description: Query Shodan across the target's IPs/orgs to enumerate open ports, banners, service versions, and exposed devices without touching the target.
items.how_to_find:
Query Shodan for exposed hosts/services:

```
shodan search 'org:"<Org>"'
shodan host <ip>
```

Look for exposed RDP/DBs/panels/old software.
items.how_to_fix: Reduce internet-exposed services (firewall/VPN), patch what must be exposed, and continuously monitor Shodan for your org. Re-query after remediation.

items.title: Cross-reference exposed hosts via Censys
items.description: Use Censys internet-wide scan data to corroborate exposed hosts, certificate associations, and service fingerprints found via Shodan.
items.how_to_find:
Cross-check exposure in Censys (certs, services):

```
# censys search 'services.tls.certificates...: <domain>' 
```
items.how_to_fix: Minimize exposed services + leaked cert metadata, and monitor Censys/Shodan continuously. Re-check exposure.

items.title: Identify CDN/WAF and hunt for origin IPs
items.description: Determine whether hosts sit behind a CDN/WAF and look for origin-IP leaks via historical DNS, SSL cert reuse, and scan-data pivots.
items.how_to_find:
Detect CDN/WAF and look for the real origin (bypasses the WAF):

```
# check via historical DNS, SPF records, CT, and Shodan favicon/cert matches
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict origin servers to accept traffic only from the CDN/WAF (IP allowlist / auth header), rotate exposed origins, and avoid leaking origin IPs in DNS/mail/history. Re-verify origin is not directly reachable.

sections.title: Domain & Infrastructure Footprinting — Technology Stack Fingerprinting

items.title: Fingerprint web technologies from public responses
items.description: Identify CMS, frameworks, web servers, JS libraries, and analytics from response headers, HTML, and public technology-detection datasets.
items.how_to_find:
Fingerprint the stack from public responses:

```
whatweb <url>
wappalyzer <url>   # or the browser extension
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: suppress unnecessary version/tech disclosure (headers, banners), and keep components patched. Re-check fingerprint.

references.title: OWASP WSTG - Fingerprint Web Application Framework

items.title: Inventory third-party SaaS and dependencies
items.description: Enumerate third-party services (payment, auth, support, marketing, CDNs) referenced in public assets to map supply-chain exposure.
items.how_to_find:
Identify SaaS/third parties from DNS, CSP, scripts, and CT:

```
curl -sI <url> | grep -i content-security-policy
# review loaded script/domains and DNS TXT verifications
```
items.how_to_fix: Maintain a third-party/SaaS inventory, assess vendor risk, and minimize what's disclosed. Re-review dependencies.

items.title: Determine hosting and cloud provider footprint
items.description: Attribute hosts to AWS/Azure/GCP/on-prem via IP ranges, certificates, and CNAME patterns to inform cloud-specific recon.
items.how_to_find:
Determine hosting/cloud from IP ownership + reverse DNS:

```
whois <ip> | grep -iE 'OrgName|netname'
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: know your cloud footprint, secure it, and minimize identifiable exposure. Re-verify footprint.

items.title: Correlate detected versions to known CVEs
items.description: Map fingerprinted component versions to public vulnerability databases to prioritize likely-vulnerable surfaces — passively, from disclosed data.
items.how_to_find:
Map fingerprinted versions to CVEs:

```
# search NVD / vulners for the detected product+version
```
items.how_to_fix: Patch/upgrade components with known CVEs, suppress version disclosure, and monitor advisories. Re-check versions after patching.

items.title: Enumerate employees from LinkedIn and professional sites
items.description: Build a roster of current employees, titles, and departments from LinkedIn and similar public professional networks.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate staff from LinkedIn/professional sites:

```
# search LinkedIn "<Company>" employees; tools like linkedin2username for name lists
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: run awareness training (staff oversharing roles/tooling), and limit disclosed org detail where possible. This informs phishing-resistance controls.

items.title: Map organizational structure and key personnel
items.description: Reconstruct reporting lines and identify high-value targets (executives, sysadmins, helpdesk) useful for social-engineering scenarios.
items.how_to_find: Map reporting structure + key personnel (execs, IT, finance) from public sources.
items.how_to_fix: Defensively: prioritize security awareness + phishing-resistant MFA for high-value roles (execs, finance, IT admins). Use to inform targeted training.

items.title: Identify technical staff and disclosed tooling
items.description: Find engineers/DevOps/security staff whose public profiles, talks, or job posts disclose internal technologies, vendors, and processes.
items.how_to_find: Identify engineers + tools they disclose (job posts, GitHub, talks, Stack Overflow) to infer the tech stack.
items.how_to_fix: Defensively: review job posts/public content that reveal internal tooling/stack, and train staff on oversharing. Use to reduce disclosure.

items.title: Compile a candidate phishing/target list
items.description: Consolidate employee data (name, role, email, location) into a prioritized list of likely social-engineering targets for the engagement scope.
items.how_to_find: Combine names + email pattern + roles into a candidate target list (for authorized phishing assessment).
items.how_to_fix: Defensively: this drives phishing simulations + training and prioritizing phishing-resistant MFA. Ensure any phishing is scoped + authorized.

sections.title: People & Organization — Email Pattern & Contact Harvesting

items.title: Derive the corporate email naming convention
items.description: Determine the email format (first.last, flast, etc.) from harvested addresses so any employee name can be converted to a probable address.
items.how_to_find:
Infer the email format (first.last@, flast@) from known addresses:

```
# cross-reference a couple of known emails; tools: hunter.io
```
items.how_to_fix: Defensively: assume the format is discoverable; focus on phishing-resistant MFA + anti-spoofing rather than obscurity. Informs target list generation.

items.title: Harvest published email addresses
items.description: Collect email addresses exposed in search engines, websites, PGP keyservers, and document metadata to seed the address list.
items.how_to_find:
Collect published addresses:

```
theHarvester -d <domain> -b all
```
items.how_to_fix: Reduce mass-published addresses (use forms/role aliases), and pair with strong anti-spoofing + filtering. Re-check what's published.

items.title: Validate addresses against breach/presence data
items.description: Check harvested addresses for existence on services and breach corpora (without active SMTP probing of the target) to confirm live accounts.
items.how_to_find:
Validate/enrich addresses against breach + presence data:

```
holehe <email>
# check breach corpora for hits
```
items.how_to_fix: Defensively: force resets + MFA for breach-exposed accounts, and monitor exposure. Use to prioritize at-risk users.

items.title: Assess email spoofing posture (SPF/DKIM/DMARC)
items.description: Review published SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records to gauge how easily the domain can be spoofed in phishing — all from public DNS.
items.how_to_find:
Check anti-spoofing records:

```
dig +short TXT <domain> | grep -i spf
dig +short TXT _dmarc.<domain>
dig +short TXT <selector>._domainkey.<domain>
```

Missing/weak `p=none` DMARC = spoofable (high).
items.how_to_fix: Publish SPF (`-all`), DKIM, and DMARC with `p=reject` (after monitoring), align records, and monitor DMARC reports. Re-check records enforce.

items.title: Enumerate employee and org social accounts
items.description: Discover official and personal social-media accounts (X, Facebook, Instagram, GitHub, etc.) tied to the org and its staff.
items.how_to_find:
Find org + employee social accounts across platforms:

```
sherlock <username>
```
items.how_to_fix: Defensively: social-media awareness training, and monitor for impersonation accounts. Use to inform employee guidance.

items.title: Extract metadata from posted media
items.description: Analyze publicly posted photos/files for EXIF/geolocation and background details that leak physical locations, badges, or screens.
items.how_to_find:
Extract metadata/geodata from posted images:

```
exiftool <image>
```

Photos can leak GPS, badges, screens, and internal layouts.
items.how_to_fix: Train staff to strip metadata + avoid posting sensitive backgrounds (badges/screens), and set social policies. Re-review posted media.

items.title: Build behavioral and interest profiles
items.description: Profile interests, schedules, and relationships from public posts to craft credible social-engineering pretexts within scope.
items.how_to_find: Aggregate posts to profile interests/routines/relationships (pretext material).
items.how_to_fix: Defensively: awareness training on oversharing that enables pretexting/spear-phishing. Use to inform training.

items.title: Hunt for sensitive disclosures in posts
items.description: Search public posts for inadvertent disclosures — screenshots with credentials, internal URLs, system names, or project details.
items.how_to_find: Search posts for accidental disclosures (screenshots of internal tools, credentials, project names).
items.how_to_fix: Train staff + monitor for sensitive disclosures, and have a takedown process. Re-check for exposed posts.

sections.title: Credential & Breach Exposure — Breach Corpora & Paste Sites

items.title: Search breach corpora for target email/domain hits
items.description: Query breach-aggregation services for accounts at the target domain that appear in known data breaches.
items.how_to_find:
Search breach data for the domain:

```
# HaveIBeenPwned domain search; check breach corpora for @<domain>
```
items.how_to_fix: Force resets + MFA for exposed accounts, subscribe to breach monitoring, and educate users on reuse. Re-check exposure.

items.title: Identify plaintext or cracked passwords in leaks
items.description: Determine whether leaked records expose usable plaintext (or already-cracked) passwords for target accounts.
items.how_to_find:
Look for plaintext/cracked passwords tied to target accounts in leak corpora.

Exposed valid passwords = critical (credential-stuffing risk).
items.how_to_fix: Force password resets for exposed accounts, enforce MFA, block reuse of breached passwords (e.g. HIBP integration), and monitor. Re-verify exposed creds are invalidated.

items.title: Monitor paste sites for leaked target data
items.description: Search paste sites (Pastebin and mirrors) for dumps containing target emails, credentials, configs, or internal data.
items.how_to_find:
Monitor paste sites for the org's data:

```
# search Pastebin/ghostbin/etc. for <domain>, project names, keys
```
items.how_to_fix: Set up continuous paste-site monitoring + alerting, and have a takedown/IR process. Re-verify monitoring is active.

items.title: Catalog exposed PII and secondary identifiers
items.description: Record exposed PII (phones, addresses, security-question answers) that supports account recovery abuse or deeper targeting.
items.how_to_find: Catalog exposed PII (phones, DOBs, security-question answers) usable for social engineering/account recovery.
items.how_to_fix: Harden account-recovery flows (don't rely on guessable PII), educate users, and monitor exposure. Use to strengthen recovery security.

items.title: Assess relevance of exposed creds to live login surfaces
items.description: Map breached credentials to the target's identified login portals (VPN, webmail, SSO) to gauge credential-stuffing risk.
items.how_to_find: Map exposed credentials to actual login surfaces (VPN, webmail, SSO, admin panels) to gauge real risk.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce MFA on all login surfaces, block breached passwords, deploy credential-stuffing protections (rate limiting, anomaly detection), and reset exposed accounts. Re-verify controls.

items.title: Identify password-reuse and reset patterns
items.description: Analyze leaked passwords for reuse across services and predictable patterns (season+year, company name) informing spray candidates.
items.how_to_find: Look for reuse/predictable-reset patterns (e.g. Season+Year, incrementing) across leaked passwords.
items.how_to_fix: Ban common/predictable + breached passwords, require MFA, and detect password spraying. Re-verify password policy.

items.title: Determine MFA coverage on exposed accounts
items.description: From public/breach signals, infer which login surfaces enforce MFA so the team understands residual risk of exposed credentials.
items.how_to_find: Determine whether exposed accounts are protected by MFA on their login surfaces.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce phishing-resistant MFA everywhere (esp. exposed accounts + privileged roles), and close MFA gaps. Re-verify MFA coverage.

items.title: Check stealer-log / infostealer exposure
items.description: Search for target accounts appearing in infostealer log dumps, which often include fresh, valid session and credential data.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether the org appears in infostealer/stealer-log dumps (session cookies + creds from infected devices):

```
# search stealer-log datasets for <domain> credentials/cookies
```

Stealer logs = active credentials + session tokens (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Treat stealer-log exposure as active compromise: reset creds, invalidate sessions/tokens, remediate the infected device, enforce MFA, and monitor. Re-verify sessions are invalidated.

sections.title: Code & Document Leakage — Public Code & Secret Leakage

items.title: Dork public repos for the org and its assets
items.description: Search GitHub/GitLab for repositories, gists, and files referencing the target's domains, internal hostnames, or product names.
items.how_to_find:
Search public code hosts for the org, domains, and internal names:

```
# github search: "<domain>" / "<internal-project>" ; github-dorks / trufflehog org scan
```
items.how_to_fix: Monitor public code hosts for org mentions, enforce a policy against pushing internal code publicly, and educate developers. Re-scan periodically.

items.title: Scan discovered repos for hardcoded secrets
items.description: Run secret scanners over public repos/history to find API keys, tokens, passwords, and private keys committed by mistake.
items.how_to_find:
Scan discovered repos for secrets:

```
trufflehog github --org=<org>
gitleaks detect --source <repo>
```

Live secrets = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Rotate any exposed secret immediately, add secret scanning + push protection, use a secrets manager, and educate devs. Re-scan to confirm remediation.

items.title: Inspect commit history and deleted content
items.description: Review full git history (not just HEAD) for secrets that were "removed" in later commits but remain recoverable from history.
items.how_to_find:
Secrets/data removed from HEAD often persist in history:

```
gitleaks detect --source <repo> --log-opts="--all"
```
items.how_to_fix: Rotate any secret ever committed (assume compromised), purge history (filter-repo/BFG) where required, and scan history in CI. Re-scan history.

references.title: GitHub - Removing sensitive data from a repository

items.title: Find leaked config and CI/CD secrets
items.description: Look for exposed config files, environment files, and CI/CD pipeline definitions that disclose endpoints, hosts, and embedded secrets.
items.how_to_find:
Look for leaked config/CI files with secrets:

```
# search for .env, *.yml, Dockerfiles, CI configs in public repos
grep -REn 'password|api[_-]?key|token|-----BEGIN' <repo>
```
items.how_to_fix: Keep config/CI secrets out of public repos (secrets manager/CI secret store), rotate exposed values, and add scanning. Re-scan.

sections.title: Code & Document Leakage — Document Metadata & Exposed Files

items.title: Discover public documents tied to the target
items.description: Find publicly hosted documents (PDF, DOCX, XLSX, PPTX) indexed in search engines or linked from the org's sites.
items.how_to_find:
Find public documents (PDF/docx/xlsx) indexed for the domain:

```
# google: site:<domain> filetype:pdf OR filetype:docx OR filetype:xlsx
```
items.how_to_fix: Review + minimize public documents, remove sensitive ones, and control indexing. Re-check indexed files.

items.title: Extract metadata (authors, software, paths)
items.description: Parse document metadata for usernames, internal file paths, software versions, printer names, and timestamps.
items.how_to_find:
Extract metadata from discovered documents:

```
exiftool <file>
# or FOCA / metagoofil across many files
```

Reveals usernames, internal paths, software versions.
items.how_to_fix: Strip metadata from published documents (sanitization in the publish pipeline), and avoid leaking usernames/paths/versions. Re-check metadata.

items.title: Identify inadvertently exposed sensitive files
items.description: Flag exposed backups, spreadsheets, and exports containing credentials, PII, or internal data that should not be public.
items.how_to_find:
Look for exposed sensitive files (backups, dumps, keys):

```
# google: site:<domain> filetype:sql OR filetype:bak OR intitle:"index of"
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove exposed sensitive files, block directory listing, restrict access, and monitor. Re-check exposure.

items.title: Build a username map from document authorship
items.description: Aggregate authorship/creator metadata across documents to derive the internal username convention and active-user list.
items.how_to_find: Compile usernames from document authorship/metadata to map the naming convention.
items.how_to_fix: Defensively: strip authorship metadata, and be aware usernames feed password-spraying. Use to inform account-security controls.

items.title: Dork for exposed files and directory listings
items.description: Use operators (site:, filetype:, intitle:index.of) to surface indexed sensitive files and open directory listings.
items.how_to_find:
Use search dorks for exposed content:

```
site:<domain> intitle:"index of"
site:<domain> ext:sql | ext:log | ext:env
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove exposed files, disable directory listing, and control indexing (robots + auth). Re-check via dorks.

items.title: Locate exposed login panels and admin interfaces
items.description: Dork for indexed admin/login/console pages (inurl:admin, intitle:login) that reveal entry points to the environment.
items.how_to_find:
Dork for admin/login panels:

```
site:<domain> inurl:admin | inurl:login | intitle:"dashboard"
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict admin/login interfaces (VPN/IP allowlist), require MFA, and avoid indexing them. Re-check exposure.

items.title: Find error messages and info disclosure via dorks
items.description: Search for indexed pages leaking stack traces, SQL errors, version strings, or debug output that disclose internals.
items.how_to_find:
Dork for error/info disclosure:

```
site:<domain> "SQL syntax" | "stack trace" | "Warning: " | "Fatal error"
```
items.how_to_fix: Return generic errors (no stack traces/paths to users), log details server-side, and de-index leaked pages. Re-check via dorks.

items.title: Mine web archives and cached pages
items.description: Use the Wayback Machine and search-engine caches to recover removed pages, old endpoints, and previously exposed data.
items.how_to_find:
Mine archives/caches for removed-but-archived content:

```
waybackurls <domain> | sort -u
gau <domain>
```
items.how_to_fix: Be aware removed content persists in archives; rotate any exposed secrets/endpoints found, and request removal where possible. Re-review archived exposure.

sections.title: Exposed Assets & Cloud — Public Cloud Storage Discovery

items.title: Enumerate candidate cloud storage buckets
items.description: Derive likely bucket/container names from the org name, domains, and products, then check public listing/read status.
items.how_to_find:
Generate + test candidate bucket names from the org/brand:

```
cloud_enum -k <org> -k <brand>
```
items.how_to_fix: Recon step. Defensively: use non-guessable bucket names, deny public access by default, and monitor. Re-enumerate to confirm none are public.

items.title: Identify publicly listable / readable buckets
items.description: Determine which discovered buckets allow anonymous listing or object read, exposing the data inside.
items.how_to_find:
Test discovered buckets for public list/read:

```
aws s3 ls s3://<bucket> --no-sign-request
curl -s https://<bucket>.s3.amazonaws.com/
```

Public listable bucket = critical data exposure.
items.how_to_fix: Enable S3 Block Public Access (account + bucket), remove public ACLs/policies, use least-privilege, and monitor. Re-test the bucket is private.

items.title: Check Azure Blob and GCS exposure
items.description: Extend bucket discovery to Azure Blob containers and Google Cloud Storage to cover multi-cloud footprints.
items.how_to_find:
Test Azure Blob + GCS for public access:

```
curl -s 'https://<account>.blob.core.windows.net/<container>?restype=container&comp=list'
gsutil ls gs://<bucket>
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable anonymous/public access on Azure containers + GCS buckets, enforce least privilege + uniform bucket-level access, and monitor. Re-test exposure.

references.title: Microsoft - Anonymous read access to blobs

items.title: Triage exposed object contents for sensitivity
items.description: Assess the nature of exposed objects (backups, source, PII, secrets) to gauge real impact of the misconfiguration.
items.how_to_find: Review any exposed objects for sensitivity (PII, secrets, backups) without exfiltrating beyond scope.
items.how_to_fix: Remove public access immediately, rotate any exposed secrets, assess breach impact, and add monitoring/DLP. Re-verify access is closed.

sections.title: Exposed Assets & Cloud — Exposed Dashboards & Dev/Staging

items.title: Find exposed dashboards and management consoles
items.description: Use scan-data search to find exposed admin dashboards (Kibana, Grafana, Jenkins, databases, Kubernetes) reachable from the internet.
items.how_to_find:
Find exposed dashboards (Grafana, Kibana, Jenkins, admin panels):

```
shodan search 'http.title:"Grafana" org:"<Org>"'
```
items.how_to_fix: Put dashboards behind VPN/SSO + MFA, remove default creds, and don't expose them publicly. Re-test exposure.

items.title: Discover dev/staging/test environments
items.description: Identify non-production environments exposed publicly (dev., staging., uat., test.) that often have weaker controls.
items.how_to_find:
Find non-prod environments (often weaker):

```
# subdomain enum for dev/staging/test/uat/qa.<domain>
```
items.how_to_fix: Protect non-prod like prod (auth/VPN, no real data), don't expose publicly, and inventory them. Re-check exposure.

items.title: Detect exposed databases and data stores
items.description: Search scan data for internet-facing databases and caches (MongoDB, Elasticsearch, Redis, etc.) bound to public interfaces.
items.how_to_find:
Find exposed databases:

```
shodan search 'product:MongoDB org:"<Org>"'   # also Elastic, Redis, etc.
```

Internet-exposed DB = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Never expose databases to the internet (firewall/VPN), require auth, enable encryption, and monitor. Re-test the DB is not reachable.

items.title: Identify exposed IoT/OT and remote-access services
items.description: Look for exposed remote-access (RDP, VNC, SSH) and IoT/OT/industrial devices attributable to the org via scan datasets.
items.how_to_find:
Find exposed IoT/OT + remote access (RDP/VNC/cameras/SCADA):

```
shodan search 'port:3389 org:"<Org>"'
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove IoT/OT + remote-access services from the internet (VPN/segmentation), require MFA, patch, and monitor. Re-test exposure.

sections.title: Exposed Assets & Cloud — Continuous Exposure Monitoring

items.title: Establish continuous attack-surface monitoring
items.description: Set up recurring discovery of new subdomains, IPs, and services so the external footprint stays current over the engagement.
items.how_to_find: Check whether external attack surface is monitored continuously vs point-in-time.
items.how_to_fix: Deploy continuous ASM (recurring subdomain/port/service discovery), alert on new exposures, and track over time. Re-verify monitoring is running.

items.title: Monitor certificate transparency for new hosts
items.description: Subscribe to CT-log alerts for the target's domains to catch newly issued certificates revealing fresh infrastructure.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether new certs (new hostnames) are monitored via CT:

```
# CT monitoring for %.<domain> (crt.sh feed / certstream)
```
items.how_to_fix: Subscribe to CT monitoring for your domains, alert on unexpected issuances/hostnames (also catches mis-issuance), and review. Re-verify monitoring.

items.title: Set up breach and credential-exposure alerts
items.description: Configure alerting on the target's domains/emails so newly published breach or paste data is flagged promptly.
items.how_to_find: Check whether breach/credential exposure is continuously monitored + alerted.
items.how_to_fix: Set up breach + credential-exposure monitoring (HIBP/stealer-log feeds), auto-alert, and tie to forced resets. Re-verify alerting.

references.title: Have I Been Pwned - Domain search / notifications

items.title: Monitor public code hosts for new secret leaks
items.description: Continuously watch GitHub/GitLab for new commits/repos referencing the org that may leak secrets or sensitive code.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether public code hosts are continuously monitored for new org secret leaks:

```
# trufflehog / GitHub secret-scanning alerts / continuous org scans
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable continuous public-code + secret-leak monitoring with auto-alert + rotation workflow, and educate devs. Re-verify monitoring is active.
